item.title: Manly Man
item.description: My family has called me "stoic, a rock, an anchor." I tend to keep my emotions bottled up, but DAMN did this movie get me bawling. I straight up had to pause it until my eyes were clear enough to keep watching. For the "literally dozens" of people who haven't seen it, I suggest you do.
item.dc:creator: JohnnyFiveAces

item.description: All of what you see here was modeled by me personally, in Blender 3D.
item.dc:creator: DacianFalx

item.title: In 1995,Pearl Jam canceled their tour due to a dispute with Ticketmaster, which began in 1992 with two free concerts in Seattle. Ticketmaster charged a fee of one dollar per ticket, which Pearl Jam found unacceptable.
item.description: In 1995, Pearl Jam canceled their tour due to a dispute with Ticketmaster, which began in 1992 with two free concerts in Seattle. Ticketmaster charged a fee of one dollar per ticket, which Pearl Jam found unacceptable. The band decided to distribute the tickets themselves. In 1993, they set a maximum price of $18 for their concerts, despite promoters suggesting triple the price. They also reduced the price of their T-shirts, giving up $2 million in revenue. In 1994, Pearl Jam declared that they would only play at venues that respected the maximum price of $18 and fees of less than $1.80. This led to a conflict with Ticketmaster, whose "service charge" ranged from $4 to $8. The band refused to give in, and because Ticketmaster controlled most of the concert venues, the 1994 summer tour was canceled, resulting in a loss of $3 million. After the release of Vitalogy in 1994, Pearl Jam announced a summer tour only at venues not affiliated with Ticketmaster, organizing everything themselves
item.dc:creator: Joyika

item.title: Shouting into the void
item.description:
Today isn't my cake day, but it is my birthday, and I'm having a rough go of it, if I'm being honest. It's not because of my age. That's never bothered me. Tomorrow will be the last time I saw or spoke to my father 4 years ago to the day. He was my hero, my best friend, and my rock. He showed me how to be a man and a father, to be empathic and caring, and to stand up for those who can't do so for themselves. I miss him terribly and haven't been able to enjoy a birthday since. I probably won't for awhile.He was mowing his lawn and was killed by someone messing with their phone while driving. He was only 59.
My mother used to call me every morning on my birthday to tell me the story of her going into labor, how her sister chose to get married that day even though my mom told her not to (she knew I was going to be born that day), and how my grandmother who was dying of breast cancer smiled her only smile that day when she was told about her first grandson. My sister tried to call me and tell me the birthday story a couple of years ago. I appreciated the gesture, but it hurt too much to be reminded that my mom couldn't tell me anymore. Mom was my other rock. She was caring, empathic, kind, full of joy, and showed me that it was always okay to be able to laugh at myself and not take myself too seriously. I miss her voice and her laugh. I lost her suddenly, as well, due to aspiration pneumonitis while she was traveling in England. She died 3 days after Mothers Day. Yes, it was seven years ago, but it still feels so fresh sometimes. She was only 55. I fully recognize that my siblings and I were blessed with two amazing, caring, and loving parents. I have two amazing kids, a wonderful relationship with my siblings, and two step-parents who love us as if we were their own. I'm just kind of in my feelings today and need to get it out. Please feel free to ignore this post and let it drift off into the ether.
item.dc:creator: J0joTheIdiotCircusBoy
